Created: 2026-06-23T10:58:06.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":4,"reply_count":0,"like_count":35,"quote_count":0,"bookmark_count":74,"impression_count":2398}
Article content: {"cover_media":"3_2069373081840467969","media_entities":["3_2069369057535750144"],"plain_text":"What This Guide Is For
This guide distills How We Think into a practical reference for product managers.
 
Read it when you need to:
Diagnose an ambiguous product problem without jumping to the first neat story.
Turn stakeholder opinions into testable questions.
Decide what evidence would change your mind.
Separate useful doubt from endless analysis.
Build team rituals that make judgment visible instead of hidden inside a PM's head.
The book is not a product management book. It is a book about disciplined thought. Its author, John Dewey, was trying to explain how people move from confusion to warranted belief. Product managers need that skill every week because the job is full of partial signals, loud opinions, unclear causes, and decisions that must be made before certainty arrives.
The short version:
Product management fails when teams treat suggestions as conclusions. How We Think teaches a better habit: pause at the point of doubt, define the real problem, form rival hypotheses, reason through what each one predicts, then test against the world.
👉 I also made it into a short addition you can drop into your agent's CLAUDE/AGENTS.md
The Core Model
Dewey's model can be translated into one product operating loop:
Something feels wrong.
The team defines what is uncertain.
Several possible explanations appear.
The team reasons through what each explanation would imply.
The team observes, tests, or ships in a way that can confirm or weaken the explanation.
The team updates its belief and carries the learning into the next decision.
For a PM, reflective thought is the path from "I think users need this" to "we have earned enough confidence to act, and we know what would prove us wrong."
The One Idea To Remember
Thinking is not the same as having thoughts.
Everyone has suggestions, hunches, opinions, and associations. Dewey cares about a narrower kind of thinking: reflective thought. Reflective thought is active, persistent, and careful. It examines a belief by looking at the grounds that support it and the consequences that follow from it.
In product terms:
A thought says, "Users seem lost."
A belief says, "The onboarding step lacks feedback, so users seem lost."
Reflective thought asks, "What evidence would show confusion? What else could explain the same behavior? If feedback is the cause, what should change after we add it?"
The important move is not becoming slower. The important move is becoming more disciplined about what counts as evidence.
The Five Phases Of Reflective Thought
1. Felt Difficulty
Reflective thought starts when smooth action breaks.
A PM notices that activation dropped, a stakeholder request does not fit the roadmap, a research quote contradicts the team's assumption, or a launch behaves differently than expected. The first signal is often discomfort. Something no longer makes sense.
The trap is to treat discomfort as a request for immediate action.
For PMs, the translation is simple:
"Users seem lost" is not yet a problem definition.
"Sales needs this feature" is not yet a problem definition.
"Activation dropped" is not yet a problem definition.
Those are felt difficulties. They tell you where to look.
The useful PM question is:
What changed, broke, surprised, slowed, or contradicted what we expected?
2. Definition Of The Difficulty
The second phase is diagnosis.
The team has to locate the problem more precisely. A vague difficulty has too many possible causes. A defined difficulty narrows the field enough that inquiry can begin.
Bad definition:
"The new onboarding is bad."
Better definition:
"Invited teammates on mobile complete account creation but do not reach the workspace because the next action is hidden below the fold."
The better version names the user slice, the situation, the broken step, and the suspected mechanism.
The useful PM question is:
Which part of the situation is uncertain?
3. Suggested Solution
Once the team defines the problem, possible solutions appear.
Dewey treats these suggestions as working ideas, not final answers. This is the move product teams often miss. A feature idea should enter the room as a hypothesis. It should not enter the room as a commitment wearing a nicer shirt.
For PMs, the translation is simple:
"Add onboarding tooltips" becomes "If users are missing the next step, then an explicit signifier near the completed action should increase continuation."
"Build a dashboard" becomes "If managers cannot coordinate because status lives in six places, then a shared status view should reduce follow-up messages."
"Add export to CSV" becomes "If finance teams need to reconcile data outside the product, then export should reduce manual copy-paste work."
The useful PM question is:
What belief would need to be true for this solution to be the right one?
4. Reasoning Through Consequences
A hypothesis earns attention only when the team reasons through what should happen if it is true.
This is where PM work gets sharper. Do not stop at "we believe X." Ask what X predicts.
If the onboarding issue is hidden next action:
Smaller screens should perform worse.
Session recordings should show users pausing or scrolling.
Support questions should mention "what now?" or "where do I go?"
Moving the action into view should improve continuation for the affected segment.
If those implications do not appear, the explanation weakens.
The useful PM question is:
If this explanation is right, what else should we see?
5. Experimental Corroboration
The final phase is contact with reality.
The team tests, observes, compares, ships, or varies conditions in a way that can strengthen or weaken the belief. A launch is not proof by itself. A launch is a test of whether predicted consequences appear.
For PMs, the translation is simple:
A discovery call tests whether the problem exists in a user's real life.
A prototype tests whether the concept makes sense before engineering investment.
An A/B test tests whether one controlled change caused a measurable difference.
A beta tests whether the workflow survives real context.
A launch tests whether the market behaves as expected at full scale.
The useful PM question is:
What observation would make us update our belief?
Key Terms For Product Managers
Reflective Thought
Active, persistent, careful consideration of a belief, including the evidence for it and the consequences that follow from it.
For PMs, use it this way:
Reflective thought is what turns "I think this matters" into "we have a reasoned bet, named assumptions, and a way to learn."
Suggestion
The first possible meaning or solution that appears in the mind.
For PMs, use it this way:
Feature requests, design ideas, roadmap themes, and stakeholder interpretations are suggestions until tested.
Belief
Something accepted as true or probable.
For PMs, use it this way:
Every roadmap item rests on beliefs about users, incentives, constraints, workflows, budgets, and outcomes.
Inference
The move from what is present to what is absent.
For PMs, use it this way:
"Users abandoned the flow, so they must be confused" is an inference. The abandonment is present. The PM infers the confusion.
Proof
Inference that has survived disciplined checking.
For PMs, use it this way:
Proof rarely means certainty. It means the team has tested the belief well enough for the decision at hand.
Observation
Purposeful search for relevant facts.
For PMs, use it this way:
Observation is not staring at dashboards. It is looking for facts that bear on the uncertainty.
Induction
Moving from particulars toward a possible general pattern.
For PMs, use it this way:
After ten customer calls, a PM forms a thesis: "The real pain is not reporting. It is reconciling data across tools."
Deduction
Taking a general idea and reasoning toward what should follow if it is true.
For PMs, use it this way:
If reconciliation is the pain, then users should already have spreadsheets, exports, manual checks, and recurring meetings around data cleanup.
Suspended Judgment
Holding a possible conclusion without accepting it too quickly.
For PMs, use it this way:
Suspended judgment means "do not commit yet." It does not mean "never decide."
Experimental Inquiry
Varying conditions to learn what causes what.
For PMs, use it this way:
Changing copy, layout, audience, and timing at once is not clean inquiry. It may move a metric, but it will not explain why.
How The Model Changes Product Work
Discovery
Discovery becomes less about collecting opinions and more about locating real difficulties.
Bad discovery asks:
"Would you use this?"
"Do you like this idea?"
"What features do you want?"
Reflective discovery asks:
"When did this last happen?"
"What did you do next?"
"What made that difficult?"
"What did you try before?"
"What would have changed the outcome?"
The goal is to see the user's situation clearly enough that the right hypothesis can form.
Roadmapping
Roadmaps become bundles of beliefs.
Each roadmap item should expose the belief underneath:
We believe this user segment has this difficulty.
We believe the difficulty matters now.
We believe the proposed work attacks the cause, not a symptom.
We believe the appetite is proportional to the risk and value.
We believe we will see these signals if the bet works.
This does not make roadmapping academic. It makes it honest.
Stakeholder Management
Stakeholder requests become evidence of pressure, not automatic evidence of user need.
When a stakeholder asks for a feature, the PM should translate the ask into inquiry:
What difficulty is this request trying to remove?
Who feels the pain?
What facts led to this request?
What would happen if we did nothing?
What alternative explanations fit the same facts?
The PM does not dismiss the stakeholder. The PM improves the request until it can be tested.
PRDs
A PRD should not merely describe what to build. It should preserve the thinking that made the build reasonable.
Add these sections:
Felt difficulty: what situation created the need?
Problem definition: what exactly is uncertain or broken?
Working hypothesis: what do we believe?
Rival hypotheses: what else might explain the facts?
Predicted consequences: what should happen if we are right?
Corroboration plan: what will we observe during and after launch?
Negative cases: where might the hypothesis fail?
This makes the PRD a thinking artifact instead of a delivery handoff.
Experiments
Experiments should isolate causes.
A weak experiment asks, "Did the metric move?"
A stronger experiment asks, "Which condition caused the movement?"
For PMs, this matters because teams often change too many things at once and then claim learning. Dewey's distinction between empirical habit and scientific inquiry cuts directly here. Habit says, "That worked last time." Scientific inquiry asks, "What condition made it work, and can we reproduce it?"
Retrospectives
A retro should extract causal learning, not just sentiment.
Replace:
What went well?
What went badly?
What should we improve?
With:
What did we expect would happen?
What happened after the change?
Which belief was strengthened?
Which belief weakened?
What negative case did we miss?
What principle can travel to the next project?
The output should be reusable judgment.
The PM's Reflective Inquiry Loop
Use this loop whenever a decision is ambiguous enough to deserve thought.
Step 1: State The Felt Difficulty
Write one sentence:
We expected [X], but observed [Y], in [situation].
Examples:
We expected invited teammates to enter the workspace, but mobile invitees stop after account creation.
We expected the new reporting page to reduce support tickets, but customers still ask for manual exports.
We expected enterprise customers to value governance, but sales calls keep returning to setup speed.
Step 2: Define The Problem
Move from vague concern to located uncertainty.
Use this format:
The problem appears to be [mechanism] affecting [specific user/context] during [specific moment], causing [observable cost].
Example:
The problem appears to be hidden next-step feedback affecting invited teammates on mobile after account creation, causing them to abandon before entering the workspace.
Step 3: Generate Rival Hypotheses
Write at least three.
For the mobile invite example:
Users do not see the next action.
Users do not understand the workspace concept.
The invite link routes them into the wrong state.
The page loads slowly enough that they leave.
They complete account creation but do not have permission to access the workspace.
Rival hypotheses prevent the first plausible story from becoming the truth by default.
Step 4: Reason Through Predictions
For each hypothesis, ask:
If this were true, what else would we expect to see?
Example:
If users do not see the next action:
Mobile screens should show lower continuation than desktop.
Session recordings should show pauses near the end of account creation.
Users may scroll, tap inactive elements, or close the tab.
Moving the next action higher should improve continuation.
Step 5: Choose The Smallest Useful Test
The test should reduce the uncertainty enough for the decision.
Options:
Run a segment analysis.
Review session replays.
Conduct five targeted customer conversations.
Search support tickets.
Run a prototype test.
Make a controlled copy or layout change.
Run a beta with explicit success criteria.
Launch with a corroboration review.
Match the rigor to the risk. Do not run a research circus for a reversible copy change. Do not use vibes for a strategic bet.
Step 6: Record The Learning
After the test, write:
What we believed.
What we observed.
What changed in our belief.
What still remains uncertain.
What principle can travel to the next decision.
Teams waste learning when they only record decisions. Record the judgment that produced the decision.
Day-To-Day PM Scenarios
Scenario 1: A VP Wants A Feature
The easy path is to accept, reject, or negotiate scope.
The reflective path starts one layer deeper:
What difficulty is the VP reacting to?
What belief makes this feature seem necessary?
What evidence supports that belief?
What else could explain the same pressure?
What small observation would sharpen the decision?
One useful response sounds like this:
"I understand the request. Before we shape it, I want to identify the difficulty it is solving. If the problem is sales enablement, the right answer might be a feature, a workflow change, or better packaging. Let us find the constraint first."
Scenario 2: Customer Research Sounds Positive
Positive interviews are dangerous because they feel like progress.
Reflective inquiry asks:
Did customers describe past behavior or future opinion?
Did they give up anything costly?
Did they show a workaround?
Did we ask for negative cases?
Did the facts support our belief, or did we hear what we hoped to hear?
One useful ritual works like this:
After every interview batch, write one section titled What would make us wrong?
Scenario 3: A Metric Drops
The easy path is to blame the most visible recent change.
Reflective inquiry separates observation from inference:
Observation: activation dropped from 42% to 34%.
Inference: the redesign caused confusion.
Rival inference: traffic mix changed.
Rival inference: a bug affected one browser.
Rival inference: a new user segment entered through a campaign.
One useful move is:
Force the team to write the top three explanations before proposing fixes.
Scenario 4: The Team Is Stuck In Analysis
Dewey does not defend endless thinking. Reflection should terminate in tested belief and action.
The PM should ask:
What decision are we delaying?
What uncertainty blocks it?
What evidence would be enough?
What is the smallest action that would produce that evidence?
What is the cost of waiting?
One useful move is:
Set a decision threshold before gathering more information.
Scenario 5: The Launch Worked, But Nobody Knows Why
The easy path is to celebrate and move on.
Reflective inquiry asks:
What did we expect?
Which expected behavior appeared?
Which unexpected behavior appeared?
Which segment drove the result?
What would reproduce this?
What principle can we use next time?
Without that step, the organization gets motion but not learning.
Learning Path For PMs
1. Learn The Difference Between Thought And Reflective Thought
Start here because the book's central distinction is easy to miss.
Exercise:
Take three product opinions you currently hold. Rewrite each one as:
I believe [claim] because [evidence]. If this is true, I would expect [prediction].
If you cannot fill in evidence or prediction, you have a suggestion, not reflective thought.
2. Learn To Notice The Felt Difficulty
PMs often inherit solution language. Train yourself to hear the difficulty underneath.
Exercise:
For five stakeholder requests, write:
What is being requested?
What difficulty might be producing the request?
What fact would help distinguish between possible difficulties?
3. Learn Suspended Judgment
Suspended judgment is the discipline of not letting the first plausible answer harden too quickly.
Exercise:
Before any meaningful product decision, require three rival hypotheses. Do not accept synonyms. Each hypothesis must imply different evidence.
4. Learn Induction And Deduction
Use induction to form a possible pattern from cases. Use deduction to test what the pattern would predict.
Exercise:
After a set of interviews, write one thesis. Then write five predictions that should be true if the thesis is right.
5. Learn Experimental Corroboration
The goal is to contact reality in a way that can change your belief.
Exercise:
For each planned experiment, answer:
What condition are we varying?
What are we holding constant?
What result would strengthen the hypothesis?
What result would weaken it?
What result would be ambiguous?
6. Learn To Store Transferable Judgment
The best learning survives the project that produced it.
Exercise:
At the end of each launch, write one reusable rule:
When [situation], users/teams/customers tend to [behavior], because [mechanism]. Next time, test [signal] before committing.
Practical Rituals And Templates
The Felt Difficulty Review
Run weekly.
Ask:
What surprised us this week?
Where did users behave differently than expected?
Which stakeholder requests point to unresolved tension?
Which support tickets repeat the same hidden cause?
Which metric moved without an explanation?
Output:
A ranked list of difficulties worth inquiry.
The Assumption Ledger
Use for any meaningful bet.
Use these columns: belief, evidence, confidence, rival explanation, prediction, next test, owner, and review date.
The ledger makes hidden judgment visible.
The Negative Case Log
Use during discovery, beta, experiments, and launch.
Capture:
Users who should have cared but did not.
Customers who adopted but churned.
Segments where the metric did not move.
Quotes that contradict the thesis.
Workarounds that solve the problem differently.
Negative cases protect the team from flattering evidence.
The PRD If-Then Section
Add this to the PRD:
If we build [solution] for [user/context], we expect [behavior/outcome] because [belief]. We will know the belief is stronger if [signal]. We will weaken the belief if [negative signal].
Example:
If we move the next action into view for invited mobile teammates, we expect more of them to enter the workspace because hidden feedback creates the current failure. We will know the belief is stronger if continuation improves for mobile invitees without a matching improvement on unaffected paths. We will weaken the belief if users still pause or abandon after the action is visible.
The Corroboration Review
Run after a launch or experiment.
Ask:
What did we predict?
What happened after release?
Which belief got stronger?
Which belief got weaker?
Which rival explanation still fits?
What should we do differently next time?
What reusable principle did we earn?
Cheat Sheet
Use this when you need the ideas quickly.
No difficulty, no inquiry.
Treat a request as a symptom until the team defines the problem.
A feature idea is a hypothesis, not a conclusion.
Suspended judgment means wait long enough to think, not wait forever.
Observation is useful only when it bears on a live uncertainty.
Induction builds a possible pattern from cases.
Deduction asks what the pattern predicts.
A test should change your confidence, not just produce a number.
Negative cases are not annoyances. They are how a belief gets sharper.
A launch should produce learning, not shipped work.
Record the reasoning, not only the decision.
Misconceptions
Misconception 1: Dewey's Model Is A Rigid Checklist
The phases are a pattern of inquiry, not a ceremony. Real thinking loops, skips, revisits, and compresses steps.
Correction prompt:
Which step matters most for the decision in front of us?
Misconception 2: More Data Means Better Thinking
Data becomes useful when it reduces a named uncertainty. A large dashboard can still be intellectual clutter.
Correction prompt:
What question is this data meant to answer?
Misconception 3: Suspended Judgment Means Indecision
Suspended judgment should move toward closure. It is a way to avoid premature certainty, not a way to avoid responsibility.
Correction prompt:
What evidence would be enough to decide?
Misconception 4: A/B Testing Automatically Means Scientific Thinking
A test is scientific only when it isolates a meaningful condition. If the team changes many things at once, the result may be useful but the cause remains muddy.
Correction prompt:
Do we know what changed the outcome?
Misconception 5: Academic Rigor Always Helps
Commercial product work has different risk levels. Some decisions need deep inquiry. Some need quick action and fast feedback.
Correction prompt:
How much uncertainty can we afford, and how reversible is this decision?
Misconception 6: Customer Quotes Are Facts
A quote is evidence of what someone said in a situation. It is not automatically evidence of what customers will do.
Correction prompt:
What behavior, cost, or prior action supports this quote?
Misconception 7: The First Cause Is Usually The Cause
The most visible cause is often the easiest story, not the best explanation.
Correction prompt:
What rival explanation fits the same facts?
Failure Modes In Product Organizations
Opinion Wearing The Costume Of Evidence
The team says "we know users need this" when it really means "a senior person believes this."
Fix:
Write the belief, the source of the belief, and what would change it.
Urgency Replacing Inquiry
The team needs a decision, so it pretends the first explanation is true.
Fix:
Separate decision speed from confidence. You can move fast while naming the uncertainty.
Discovery As Compliment Collection
The team counts enthusiasm as evidence.
Fix:
Look for past behavior, costly commitment, workarounds, and negative cases.
Experiments That Cannot Teach
The team changes too many conditions and learns only that "something worked."
Fix:
Name the variable you are testing before the test starts.
PRDs That Hide Reasoning
The document explains the solution but not the thought that made the solution reasonable.
Fix:
Add beliefs, rival hypotheses, predictions, and corroboration plan.
Retros That Store Sentiment Instead Of Judgment
The team records what felt good or bad, then loses the causal learning.
Fix:
End every retro with one transferable principle.
Endless Inquiry
The PM keeps adding research because deciding feels uncomfortable.
Fix:
Define the decision threshold and stop when the threshold is met.
Operating Checklist
Before committing to a meaningful product decision, ask:
What is the felt difficulty?
Have we defined the problem before naming the solution?
What belief are we treating as true?
What rival explanations could fit the same facts?
What evidence would change our mind?
What negative cases have we written down?
What should happen if our idea is right?
What would happen if our idea is wrong?
What test or launch will corroborate or weaken it?
Is this decision risky enough to deserve more inquiry?
Is this decision reversible enough to move faster?
Have we reached a decision, or are we hiding in analysis?
A Full PM Example
Imagine a team sees activation drop after a redesign.
The quick story:
"The redesign is bad."
The reflective path:
Felt Difficulty
Activation dropped from 42% to 34% after the redesign.
Defined Difficulty
The drop appears only for invited teammates on mobile. They finish account creation but do not enter the workspace.
Rival Hypotheses
The next action is below the fold on mobile.
The invite link routes some users to the wrong state.
Users do not understand that account creation is separate from workspace entry.
Page load time is causing abandonment.
A permission bug blocks access after signup.
Reasoned Predictions
If the next action is hidden:
Mobile should underperform desktop.
Session recordings should show pauses, scrolling, or taps near the bottom of the screen.
Moving the next action higher should improve continuation for mobile invitees.
If routing is wrong:
Affected users should land on an unexpected page.
The issue should cluster around invite links.
Reproducing the invite flow should expose inconsistent state.
If the concept is unclear:
Users should ask what the workspace is.
Copy changes should help more than layout changes.
Similar confusion may appear on desktop too.
Test
Review mobile invite sessions, reproduce the invite flow, segment continuation by device and invite state, and ship the smallest visible-next-action fix if the first hypothesis holds.
Updated Belief
The conclusion should not be "the redesign failed." The useful conclusion is narrower:
Mobile invitees lose the next action after account creation because the workspace-entry CTA is not visible at the moment they expect confirmation and direction.
That belief can now shape the fix.
How To Use This Reference
Use it as a thinking aid at four moments:
Before Discovery
Define the felt difficulty and the assumptions that need testing.
Before Roadmap Commitment
Turn each candidate item into a belief, prediction, and test.
Before PRD Review
Check whether the document explains why the work should exist, not only what should be built.
After Launch
Compare predicted consequences with observed behavior and record the transferable learning.","preview_text":"What This Guide Is For
This guide distills How We Think into a practical reference for product managers.
 
Read it when you need to:
Diagnose an ambiguous product problem without jumping to the first","entities":{"urls":[{"text":"https://abs.twimg.com/emoji/v2/svg/1f449.svg"},{"text":"https://gist.github.com/gnurio/22aad249d74e4a38b742510af35b2bc9"}]},"title":"How We Think: A Practical Guide for PM Work"}
